_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years. An upward-sloping curve indicates higher yields for longer maturities, reflecting normal economic conditions and expectations of growth and inflation. An inverted curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded US recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.63% with a positive 10Y-2Y spread of 0.36 points, indicating the curve has re-steepened above zero after spending 2022-2024 inverted, reversing the deepest inversion point of 0.0 points.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread—the difference between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields—is a widely followed recession indicator. Its inversion, when the spread falls below zero, has preceded all modern recessions, signaling market expectations of economic slowdown. Following a prolonged inversion from 2022 through 2024, the spread has reverted to a positive 0.36 points, suggesting reduced immediate recession risk. The New York Fed's recession probability, currently at 31%, aligns with this cautiously moderating outlook, though the yield curve's un-inversion often occurs prior to recession onset, warranting careful interpretation.
notes.now: The current yield curve shape, with a positive 0.36 points 2s10s spread and a 10-year yield at 4.63%, indicates a reduced but non-negligible risk of recession. Combined with the NY Fed’s 31% recession probability and historical patterns, the market signals a complex, evolving economic environment rather than a definitive near-term downturn.
